title: No Doors on the Main Street
summary: Interviewed in Montgomery, Alabama on June 1, 2011 by Joe Mosnier and filmmaker John Bishop, Gwendolyn M. Patton paints her arrival at Tuskegee in the fall of 1961: a large ornate campus where the buildings on Campus Avenue had no front entrances facing the main thoroughfare, and the Lincoln Gate named for Abraham Lincoln.

title: He Built the Doors Facing the Campus
summary: Patton recalls the National Guard greeting them, called up because the city schools were being integrated, and learning that the doorless thoroughfare was Booker T. Washington's deliberate choice, turning every entrance inward to the campus so Black and white passersby would not feel welcomed, which proved to her he was a race man despite being called an uncle Tom.

title: The Father of Black Power
summary: She describes her childhood paper on George Washington Carver written to dispel the peanut-man myth, her father Clarence Robert Patton Sr. as a race man, and the story of Robert Russa Moton insisting on Black professional staff at the new VA hospital for World War I veterans against Woodrow Wilson's directive that only menial roles go to Black workers.

title: The Veterans Lined the Highway
summary: Patton tells how Moton called out the Black World War I veterans who lined the highway to the VA hospital when the Klan threatened violence, and the Klan never showed, and connects this to a 1967 piece she wrote in the hospital calling Booker T. Washington the father of Black power and the on-campus Tuskegee Black Power Conference organized by SNCC and underwritten by SHARP.

part: The Freedom Riders Come to Montgomery
title: The Mother's Day Massacre
summary: Patton recounts how, as a race-conscious young student in 1961, she stayed connected to Montgomery when the Freedom Riders came through, what she calls the Mother's Day Massacre, and how the movement infrastructure including a secretary to Dr. King and the Montgomery Improvement Association jumped into gear to rescue and shelter the riders.

title: They Came and Got Me
summary: She describes the family divisions over involvement, status-conscious schoolteacher aunts versus the broader-minded Aunt Mary, her grandparents the Pattons who worked at the Greyhound station coming to take her to the all-night mass meeting, the teenagers feeling invincible amid white men she took for newspaper people, and a youth named John Lacy crawling through the basement window to make phone calls.

title: They Are Not to Enter My Front Door
topic: Aunt Chick and the White Boy
summary: Patton recalls hosting an integrated group of Freedom Riders at her Aunt Chick's home the next day, and her aunt taking her to the kitchen to declare that as long as she had to use the back door of white folks' houses, no white person would enter the front door of her home, a distinction between houses and home Patton had to take under consideration even as a race person.

title: Up the Rickety Back Steps
summary: Patton describes entering the segregated Benson Sanatorium in Lafayette, Alabama in January 1963 to treat tuberculosis in her lungs, arriving weak by ambulance from Tuskegee's John Andrew Memorial Hospital, made to use the rickety back steps and a back vestibule that served as the intake room, traumatized by a disease she had known only as a deadly plague.

title: The Wards Were Horrifying
summary: She maps the Black women's section: a three-bed room she was first placed in, two huge wards of eight to ten patients each, a tiny black-and-white TV, and the daily shots over six months as she fought to regain strength, finding the crowded wards horrifying and being made to clean their own areas, which she took at first for part of the therapy.

title: The Movement Is Wherever You Are
summary: Patton tells of befriending a deathly-ill mother and her little girl, watching the March on Washington in August 1963 on the tiny TV the state could cut off at will, then the Birmingham church bombing that killed the four little girls in September, and resolving that the movement is wherever you are, making the sanatorium her movement base.

title: Chopin in the Sitting Room
summary: She recounts seeking the sanatorium's library, taking the little girl by the hand into the white section's game room where a baby grand piano stood, and playing Chopin's C-sharp minor to the stunned and angry white patients, then stopping when she realized she risked being a mammy performing for a white child at the expense of her own.

title: I Would Like to See the Menu
topic: Demanding the Library and the Food
summary: Patton describes negotiating with the head nurse, who reminded her of the matron in One Flew Over the Cuckoo's Nest, to arrange Jim Crow library visits for herself and the child, then challenging the monotonous boiled food by asking to see the kitchen menu, until the staff began posting it and she could choose perch and encourage fellow patients to do likewise.

title: Way Over Yonder Where Jesus Is
summary: She remembers the patient-led outdoor chapel services under a pecan tree with sorrowful death-themed hymns like Way Over Yonder Where Jesus Is, and reflects that the white staff tolerated and even respected her because they assumed Black people ignorant, were impressed by her intelligence and determination, knew she was temporary, and feared reprisal from her well-connected family.

title: One Big Fat Disease
summary: Patton describes returning to Tuskegee in the fall of 1964, where a roommate terrified of tuberculosis refused to share a room, so the school moved the roommate and left Patton alone in Travis Hall, the honor dorm, and how the Greensquare community embraced her, her family pointedly drinking from her glass, and her grandmother Flora and Aunt Jix supporting her.

title: Never for Integration, Ever
summary: She found the visiting SNCC women tough, committed, and resolute, but worried she thought too deeply because she had never believed in integration, only desegregation, equal opportunity, and justice, recalling that her Montgomery teachers had pushed her toward the University of Alabama assuming her Northern background, and she had refused, wanting only to be the world's best cheerleader.

title: It Was About Sitting Anywhere You Please
summary: Patton recounts how her grandmother, a block captain in the bus boycott, always sat in the back of the bus while young Gwendolyn sat up front to aggravate white folks, and how her grandmother explained that the boycott was not about sitting next to white people but about sitting anywhere you please, and she was pleased to sit there, a lesson that altered Patton's whole thought process.

title: It Ain't About Integration
topic: The Class Problem in the Caste
summary: She explains how watching the desegregation of Macon County schools, where middle-class professional children were bused to the white school downtown while poor Black families already lived nearby, and seeing the Negro swimming pool reserve professional children's days separate from working-class children's days, showed her the class problem within the caste and deepened her skepticism of integration for its own sake.

title: You Should Not Submerge the People
summary: Patton describes the founding of the Tuskegee Institute Advancement League (TIAL) in early 1965 and her conviction, drawn from the Montgomery Improvement Association's refusal to become an SCLC chapter and surrender the identity that sustained the 381-day boycott, that an organizer must lift up a grassroots group's own identity and structure rather than subsume it.

title: Two Christmas Parties
summary: She recalls Tuskegee as a bourgeois near-prep school with a campus laundry, where President Dr. Foster held one Christmas party for the maintenance and help and another for the professional and VA staff, and how, becoming close to Foster, she argued the cost-effectiveness of a single party until the businessman PhD began to reason with her and they nearly became peers.

title: Tuskegee Had Become a Liberated Zone
summary: Patton describes Tuskegee by early 1965 as a liberated zone where SNCC members attended classes and ate in the dining hall and the administration had given up, with faculty support from progressive white professors Dr. Foster had hired and Black professors turning nationalist, as students went to Lowndes and Dallas counties to organize before the Selma to Montgomery march.

title: Education Has to Be Relevant
summary: She explains the conviction that education must serve the community or a degree means nothing, fed by compulsory Negro history and assigned reading of W.E.B. Du Bois and Booker T. Washington that was meant to change behavior toward the revolutionary, and recounts writing her sophomore paper on homosexuality after the card catalog offered only deviant behavior, prompted by her cousin Bobby's love for a neighborhood friend.

title: You Ain't Deviant to Me
summary: Patton tells how a friend named Junior, married after fathering a son, confided he was homosexual and pointed her to the Mattachine Press and San Francisco and a book she likened to a Bible of the gay world, leading her to learn the terms Lesbos and lesbian, and reflects that SNCC people in the heat of the day did not do this kind of contemplation.

title: Who Are You, Where Do You Come From
summary: She names the TIAL circle of enrolled Tuskegee students including George Ware of Buffalo, George Davis of Rhode Island, Sammy Younge Jr., Ruby Taylor of the Alabama Black Belt, and Kathleen Neal Cleaver, a group of broad perspective that held philosophical discussions about identity and lifestyle, contrasting them with SNCC, caught up in tactical, day-to-day grassroots drudgery.

title: How Dare You Got Your Degree
summary: Patton recounts Stokely Carmichael, impressed by Tuskegee students yet wanting everyone to drop out and join SNCC, and her retort that he had gone back to Howard for his own degree, and her practical argument, made also to Bettina Aptheker who was horrified by the campus ROTC, that many Tuskegee students needed ROTC for scholarships and the school ran strong draft and conscientious-objector counseling.

part: The March That Wouldn't Turn Around
title: We Thought It Would Take Three Days
summary: Patton describes meeting as TIAL direct-action chair with James Forman, Hosea Williams, and Dr. King at Birmingham's A.G. Gaston Motel, the democratic decision to march from Selma until they reached Montgomery, and returning to organize the East Black Belt across Macon and Bullock counties with Sammy Younge taking the lead stopping cars on Campus Avenue to raise money.

title: Every Hour on the Hour
summary: She explains TIAL's meticulous logistics, gridding cars so students could picket downtown every hour without missing class, which astonished SNCC and made James Forman the campus mentor while Stokely went to Lowndes County, and recounts watching Bloody Sunday on television, horrified, then gearing up to meet the marchers in Montgomery on what they expected to be the third day.

title: He Promised the Children
summary: Patton tells of her fury when Dr. King prayed and turned the march around on the Tuesday, a breach of the collective decision that felt to the youngsters like a broken promise, then Judge Frank M. Johnson Jr.'s injunction, and Dr. Foster calling her in, thinking her malleable, to show her the injunction paper, which she pocketed without reading because the caravan and buses were already organized.

title: It Said Nothing About Macon County
summary: She recounts deposing the New York student-body president who would not serve the movement and installing George Ware, mobilizing thirty or forty cars and paid buses with cafeteria workers ready to strike, and how George Ware and George Davis read that the injunction barred only the Selma-to-Montgomery march and said nothing about a march coming from Macon County, so they could play the legal angle.

title: The Window From Italy
summary: Patton digresses into Montgomery church history: First Baptist that sheltered the Freedom Riders in 1961 with its Italian windows and pulpit installed by the race man Reverend Stokes, whose likeness sits above John the Baptist in the glass, and the offshoot Dexter Avenue Baptist Church, divided by class feuds between laborers her grandfather housed and status-conscious schoolteachers.

title: Fifteen Hundred to Montgomery
topic: Taking Refuge in the Cold Church
summary: She tells how, of Tuskegee's roughly 2,300 students, about 1,500 were mobilized democratically by stomping feet in the dining hall to call a mass meeting, and how, after the governor refused them and the cold rainy night set in, they took refuge in Dr. King's Dexter Avenue Baptist Church, finding the heat off, the lights dark, and the toilets unable to flush.

title: We're Going to Take Over This
summary: Patton recounts learning the deacons had cut the church's utilities, deciding to occupy and hold it as Alabama State students arrived to buffer them from the police, and a heated debate in which the older graduate student George Ware grew protective and called Forman a manipulator of the undergraduates, while Patton insisted the students were grown scholars who knew the risks, a stance that helped get her elected student-body president.

title: The March That Wouldn't Turn Around
summary: She explains that to stay true to their word that it was a march, students led by Sammy Younge Jr. ran a relay in which people got out of the caravan to walk and the cars behind picked them up, and that the name the march that wouldn't turn around was coined years later by a New Mexico student who researched her papers in the state archives and won a history award.

title: Selma to Lowndes to Montgomery
summary: Patton argues the Selma-to-Montgomery story overlooks Lowndes County, the longest stretch, and recounts her concern as an organizer for how the marchers would eat and relieve themselves, persuading Dr. Foster, who fed 2,300 students daily, to provide barbecue chicken for a campsite where she had tasted the poor food, taking it as a sign Foster grasped the depth of their mission.

title: Now What Do We Do With This Vote
summary: She describes the rich movement history of proud Lowndes County and the discussions with TIAL and James Forman that drew the distinction between integration and desegregation leading to self-determination, and how, with the Voting Rights Act won, the Lowndes County people led by John Hulett chose to seek offices closest to the people rather than mayor or city council.

title: Closest to the People
summary: Patton explains why Lowndes County people wanted the sheriff who came unrobed into the Black community, the board of education for decent schools, the tax assessor who had over-assessed Black landowners while white plantations were taxed nearly nothing, and the coroner who ruled Black deaths in jail and ditches suicide, and why, knowing the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party, they refused to replicate that model or chase delegates to distant Atlantic City.

title: No Clouds in the Sky
summary: She recounts the November 1966 election the people of Lowndes County saw stolen through white terror, tenant farmers hauled to the polls by plantation owners and reprisals against people who had only their lives to lose, described in her piece No Clouds in the Sky, contrasted with neighboring Macon County, which elected Black sheriff Lucius Amerson to the consternation of the Black middle class.

title: We Didn't Hit Anything
summary: Patton describes the eve of the first Black Power Conference underwritten by SHARP, which Dr. Foster moved off campus until Dean Phillips arranged a Boy Scout camp site, and the mysterious February 10, 1967 car accident as she drove a young man toward her grandmother's home to lay over before flying to Canada to avoid the draft, an accident in which nothing struck them yet she woke at John Andrew Memorial Hospital among the SNCC people.

title: Have You Talked to the Lord About It
summary: She tells how Dr. Hume, a bourgeois doctor who hated the movement, wanted to amputate her leg, and how, motivated partly by a young woman's thoughts of intimacy, she asked whether he had sought a second opinion or talked to the Lord, then summoned her devout paternal grandparents, who dispatched her cousin to take her from the hospital because the doctor was a heathen.

part: A World Opened in New York
title: I Will Work for SNCC
summary: Patton closes on calling Bettina Aptheker to find a doctor and her move to New York, where SNCC and CORE people, James Forman, her fiance, Julia Wright from Paris, and the Communist, Socialist Workers, and Progressive Labor parties supported her, and where she met Mary Kochiyama and Puerto Ricans for the first time, deciding on furlough from long hospital stays that she would work for SNCC.
